title: Whether neighbourhoods should ban short-term rentals
summary: Argue whether residential neighbourhoods should ban or strictly cap short-term holiday rentals (Airbnb, booking platforms) to protect long-term residents, or accept that the income matters too much to outlaw.
topic: Whether neighbourhoods should ban or strictly cap short-term rentals
prompt: Write a 350–500-word argumentative essay about whether residential neighbourhoods in cities like Lisbon, Krakow, Hue, or Buenos Aires should ban short-term holiday rentals (Airbnb and equivalent platforms) — or strictly cap them — to protect long-term residents, or whether the income matters too much to outlaw. Anchor in a specific neighbourhood. Use a 'what' cleft, a concession ('Granted,'), at least one passive construction, and one participle clause. Take a clear position. Avoid blanket Airbnb-bashing.
hintStarters: Walking through Alfama on a hot Lisbon afternoon, you notice that almost every doorway has the small key-box of a short-term rental fixed to it, and that the only residents still living year-round are…
What a short-term rental does to a neighbourhood, beyond the obvious churn of suitcases on cobblestones, is something the platforms have spent fifteen years…
Granted, the income that a flat in Alfama generates as a holiday rental can be the difference between an elderly owner staying in the neighbourhood and being…

hintVocab.meaningVi: miễn trừ cho các nhà vận hành đã có
bảo vệ người thuê dài hạn

hintVocab.phrase: wheelie suitcase at four in the morning
hintVocab.meaningVi: vali kéo lúc bốn giờ sáng

sampleText:
Walking through Alfama on a hot Lisbon afternoon, you notice that almost every doorway has the small key-box of a short-term rental fixed to it, and that the only residents still living year-round are an elderly couple who refuse to leave and the family on the corner whose grandmother's name is still on the deed. The bakery has become a wine bar with English signage. The grocer has become a souvenir shop. At four in the morning, a wheelie suitcase rolls past on the cobblestones, then another, and the few sleeping locals turn over in their beds.

What a short-term rental does to a neighbourhood, beyond the obvious churn of suitcases on cobblestones, is something the platforms have spent fifteen years politely declining to investigate themselves. They believe they are democratising tourism. They are also, in measurable ways, redistributing housing — removing flats from the long-term market, raising rents for the residents who remain, and gradually converting the streets of the centre from places where people live into places where people stay.

Granted, the income that a flat in Alfama generates as a holiday rental can be the difference between an elderly owner staying in the neighbourhood and being forced to sell to a developer. Supplementary rental income from a spare flat or a top-floor room has, for some families, replaced pensions the state never adequately paid. The accusation that everyone running a short-term rental is a faceless speculator is empirically false, and policy made in that spirit punishes exactly the small operators it should protect.

Even so, the argument that the market should be left to sort itself out is no longer credible to anyone who has watched Alfama go through the past decade. Once a critical mass of flats is converted, prices for the remaining long-term flats rise sharply, the kind of small shops that sustain residential life become unaffordable, and the neighbourhood is, within five years, no longer a neighbourhood. The bakery does not return when the law catches up. The grocer does not come back when the cap is finally imposed. What is lost is not recoverable by the same regulation that ought to have prevented it.

The defensible position is a real cap rather than a vague ban — a permit limit set per street, with existing small operators grandfathered in, and new permits issued only when the long-term resident population drops below a named threshold. A street belongs first to the people who live there. A city that cannot say that out loud, and write it into law, is leaving its central neighbourhoods to be quietly converted into hotels with worse plumbing and no front desk. The hosts who stay are then asked to live in the hotel.

sampleGrammarNotes.title: 'What' cleft to expose the platforms' real effect
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyVi: 'What a short-term rental does to a neighbourhood, beyond the obvious churn of suitcases on cobblestones, is something the platforms have spent fifteen years politely declining to investigate themselves' dùng cấu trúc 'What … is …' với cụm chèn ('beyond the obvious churn…') để loại bỏ câu trả lời bề nổi và sau đó đưa vào một lời buộc tội nhẹ ('politely declining to investigate themselves'). Đây là cách tinh tế để công kích một thiết chế.
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyEn: 'What a short-term rental does to a neighbourhood, beyond the obvious churn of suitcases on cobblestones, is something the platforms have spent fifteen years politely declining to investigate themselves' uses a what-cleft with an inserted phrase ('beyond the obvious churn…') to rule out the surface answer and then deliver a soft accusation ('politely declining to investigate themselves'). It is a subtle way to indict an institution.

sampleGrammarNotes.title: Passive 'is converted' / 'is imposed' to background regulators and platforms
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyVi: 'Once a critical mass of flats is converted' và 'the grocer does not come back when the cap is finally imposed' dùng passive để mô tả các quá trình hệ thống mà không cần đặt chủ thể chịu trách nhiệm vào câu. Cấu trúc này cho phép tác giả mô tả ảnh hưởng có cấu trúc — không quy về một cá nhân — trong khi vẫn để cho lập luận về hậu quả có sức nặng.
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyEn: 'Once a critical mass of flats is converted' and 'the grocer does not come back when the cap is finally imposed' use the passive to describe systemic processes without placing the responsible party in subject position. The structure lets the writer describe structural effects — not blamed on any one person — while keeping the consequence argument forceful.

sampleTranslationVi:
Đi bộ qua Alfama vào một buổi chiều Lisbon nóng nực, bạn nhận ra rằng hầu như mọi ngưỡng cửa đều có hộp đựng chìa khóa nhỏ của một căn cho thuê ngắn hạn gắn trên đó, và rằng những cư dân duy nhất còn sống quanh năm là một cặp vợ chồng cao tuổi từ chối rời đi và gia đình ở góc phố mà tên bà ngoại của họ vẫn còn trên giấy chủ quyền. Tiệm bánh đã trở thành một quán rượu vang với biển hiệu tiếng Anh. Cửa hàng tạp hóa đã trở thành một cửa hàng lưu niệm. Vào lúc bốn giờ sáng, một chiếc vali kéo lăn qua trên đá lát, rồi một chiếc nữa, và vài người dân địa phương đang ngủ trở mình trên giường.

Điều mà một căn cho thuê ngắn hạn làm với một khu phố, vượt ra ngoài sự xoay vòng hiển nhiên của vali trên đá lát, là điều mà các nền tảng đã dành mười lăm năm lịch sự từ chối điều tra chính mình. Họ tin rằng họ đang dân chủ hóa du lịch. Họ cũng đang, theo những cách có thể đo lường được, phân phối lại nhà ở — loại bỏ các căn hộ khỏi thị trường dài hạn, đẩy giá thuê cho các cư dân còn lại, và dần dần chuyển đổi các con phố ở trung tâm từ những nơi người ta sống thành những nơi người ta ở tạm.

Công nhận, thu nhập mà một căn hộ ở Alfama tạo ra như một căn cho thuê kỳ nghỉ có thể là sự khác biệt giữa một chủ sở hữu cao tuổi ở lại khu phố và bị buộc phải bán cho một nhà đầu tư phát triển. Thu nhập cho thuê bổ sung từ một căn hộ trống hay một phòng tầng trên cùng đã, đối với một số gia đình, thay thế lương hưu mà nhà nước không bao giờ trả đủ. Cáo buộc rằng mọi người vận hành cho thuê ngắn hạn là một nhà đầu cơ vô danh thì sai về mặt thực nghiệm, và chính sách được làm theo tinh thần đó trừng phạt chính xác những nhà vận hành nhỏ mà nó nên bảo vệ.

Dẫu vậy, lập luận rằng thị trường nên được để tự sắp xếp không còn đáng tin cậy đối với bất cứ ai đã chứng kiến Alfama trải qua thập kỷ vừa qua. Một khi một khối lượng tới hạn các căn hộ được chuyển đổi, giá cho các căn hộ dài hạn còn lại tăng mạnh, loại cửa hàng nhỏ duy trì đời sống cư dân trở nên không thể chi trả, và khu phố, trong vòng năm năm, không còn là một khu phố. Tiệm bánh không quay lại khi luật bắt kịp. Cửa hàng tạp hóa không quay lại khi giới hạn cuối cùng được áp dụng. Cái bị mất thì không thể phục hồi được bằng cùng một quy định lẽ ra phải ngăn chặn nó.

Lập trường có thể bảo vệ được là một giới hạn thực sự thay vì một lệnh cấm mơ hồ — một giới hạn giấy phép đặt theo từng con phố, với các nhà vận hành nhỏ hiện hữu được miễn trừ, và giấy phép mới chỉ được cấp khi dân số cư dân dài hạn giảm xuống dưới một ngưỡng đã nêu. Một con phố trước hết thuộc về những người sống ở đó. Một thành phố không thể nói điều đó lớn lên, và viết nó vào luật, đang để các khu phố trung tâm của mình lặng lẽ bị chuyển đổi thành khách sạn với hệ thống ống nước tệ hơn và không có quầy lễ tân. Các chủ nhà ở lại sau đó được yêu cầu sống trong khách sạn.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence uses a 'what' cleft to expose what short-term rentals really do?
options: What a short-term rental does to a neighbourhood, beyond the obvious churn of suitcases on cobblestones, is something the platforms have spent fifteen years politely declining to investigate themselves.
The platforms have spent fifteen years what a short-term rental does to a neighbourhood politely declining to investigate themselves.
Politely declining to investigate themselves what a short-term rental does is something the platforms have spent to a neighbourhood fifteen years.

Beyond the obvious churn of suitcases on cobblestones is something the platforms have spent fifteen years what a short-term rental does to a neighbourhood politely declining to investigate themselves.
mcQuestions.explanation: The cleft 'What … is …' with an inserted phrase rules out the surface answer (the noise) and indicts the platforms ('politely declining to investigate themselves').

mcQuestions.prompt: What does 'to hollow out a street' mean?
options: To dig up the road for repairs
To gradually empty a residential street of its long-term residents and the small businesses that depend on them
To make a street physically narrower
To remove the cobblestones
mcQuestions.explanation: Hollowing out describes the slow loss of residential density and the neighbourhood economy that depended on it — a structural decay underneath an outwardly busy surface.
mcQuestions.hint: What does 'hollow' mean as a noun, and what would 'hollowing out' do to a street?

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence correctly uses 'Granted,' as a concession opener?
options: Granted, the income that a flat in Alfama generates as a holiday rental can be the difference between an elderly owner staying in the neighbourhood and being forced to sell to a developer.
The income granted that a flat in Alfama generates as a holiday rental can be the difference between an elderly owner staying in the neighbourhood and being forced to sell to a developer.
Being forced to sell to a developer granted the income that a flat in Alfama generates as a holiday rental can be the difference between an elderly owner staying.
Holiday rental can be the difference granted the income between an elderly owner staying in the neighbourhood and being forced to sell to a developer that a flat in Alfama generates as a.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Granted,' followed by a complete clause is the cleanest B2 concession opener.
mcQuestions.hint: Which option places 'Granted,' before a full sentence?

mcQuestions.prompt: Which passive construction backgrounds the developers and platforms?
options: Once a critical mass of flats is converted, prices for the remaining long-term flats rise sharply.
A critical mass of flats once prices for the remaining long-term flats is converted rise sharply.
Sharply rise prices for the remaining long-term flats a critical mass of flats once is converted.
Is converted a critical mass of flats once rise sharply prices for the remaining long-term flats.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'is converted' is a passive construction that puts the flats in subject position and hides the developers/owners doing the converting.
mcQuestions.hint: Which option uses 'is converted' as a passive verb?

options: Inherited from one's grandfather
Exempted from a new regulation because one was already operating before the regulation took effect
Voted in by elderly relatives
Replaced by one's grandfather
mcQuestions.explanation: Grandfathering is the policy device of letting existing operators continue under the old rules while applying new restrictions to newcomers — the essay uses it to protect small operators from a blanket ban.
mcQuestions.hint: What does it mean for an existing user to be 'grandfathered in' to a new law?

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence opens with a participle clause to set the analytical scene?
options: Walking through Alfama on a hot Lisbon afternoon, you notice that almost every doorway has the small key-box of a short-term rental fixed to it.
You notice walking through Alfama on a hot Lisbon afternoon that almost every doorway has the small key-box of a short-term rental fixed to it.
Has the small key-box of a short-term rental fixed to it walking through Alfama on a hot Lisbon afternoon almost every doorway you notice.
Lisbon afternoon on a hot walking through Alfama you notice almost every doorway has the small key-box of a short-term rental fixed to it.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Walking through Alfama on a hot Lisbon afternoon' is a present-participle clause opener that sets the analytical scene before the main clause delivers the observation.
mcQuestions.hint: Look for an -ing phrase at the start of the sentence, separated by a comma from the main clause.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which transition best signals a return to the main argument after a concession?
options: For example, all Airbnb hosts are evil.
Even so, the argument that the market should be left to sort itself out is no longer credible to anyone who has watched Alfama go through the past decade.
On the contrary, no neighbourhood has ever lost a single resident.
Furthermore, all short-term rentals are heroic.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Even so' grants the previous concession (small operators are real) and pivots back to the writer's main argument that market-only reasoning has failed.
mcQuestions.hint: Which phrase both accepts the concession and reasserts the writer's broader position?

options: Permanent residency in a foreign country
Short-term, temporary stays of guests who do not live in the building year-round
Visiting a neighbourhood for one day
Occupying a building illegally
mcQuestions.explanation: Transient occupancy is the legal and planning term for guest-style stays — the rotating, short-duration use that defines short-term rentals.
mcQuestions.hint: What does 'transient' mean as an adjective?

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence maintains the most formal academic register?
options: Honestly, Airbnb is just basically destroying neighbourhoods, no question.
It's kind of obvious that short-term rentals should be banned everywhere, full stop.

The unrestricted growth of short-term rental platforms in dense central neighbourhoods has been associated with measurable decreases in long-term residency and corresponding increases in median rents.
Airbnb is like, you know, kind of ruining cities and stuff.
mcQuestions.explanation: Option C uses formal vocabulary ('unrestricted growth', 'measurable decreases', 'median rents') and avoids contractions, slang, and informal markers.

mcQuestions.prompt: What is the essay's central position?
options: All short-term rentals should be banned everywhere immediately.
No regulation of short-term rentals is ever justified.

A street-level permit cap with existing small operators grandfathered in, and new permits issued only when long-term residency drops below a named threshold, is preferable both to a vague ban and to a free market that has demonstrably damaged residential neighbourhoods.
Tourists should be banned from Alfama entirely.
mcQuestions.explanation: The essay rejects both blanket bans and laissez-faire in favour of a specific, calibrated cap with named protections.
mcQuestions.hint: Look at paragraph five for the writer's specific policy proposal.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which option best paraphrases 'converting the streets of the centre from places where people live into places where people stay'?
options: Building more residential housing in the city centre
Transforming central neighbourhoods from inhabited residential blocks into rotating accommodation for visitors — short-term presence replacing long-term life
Moving residents from the centre to the suburbs
Encouraging more long-term tenancies
mcQuestions.explanation: The phrase contrasts 'live' (long-term residency, neighbourhood) with 'stay' (transient occupancy) — a single-word switch that captures the structural change.
mcQuestions.hint: What is the difference between 'living' somewhere and 'staying' somewhere?

mcQuestions.prompt: What does 'critical mass' mean as the essay uses it?
options: A religious service for critics
A threshold quantity beyond which a process becomes self-reinforcing — here, the number of short-term rentals beyond which a neighbourhood begins to lose long-term residential life rapidly
A serious mass of complaints
The largest possible group of people
mcQuestions.explanation: Critical mass is the tipping-point quantity; once enough flats are converted, the neighbourhood's residential economy can no longer sustain itself and decline accelerates.
mcQuestions.hint: What does 'critical' mean in physics, and what tipping point is the essay describing?

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence uses a defining relative clause correctly?
options: The kind of small shops that sustain residential life become unaffordable.
Sustain residential life the kind of small shops that become unaffordable.
Unaffordable become the kind of small shops that sustain residential life.
Residential life sustain the kind of small shops that become unaffordable.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'that sustain residential life' is a defining relative clause specifying exactly which small shops the essay means.
mcQuestions.hint: Defining relative clauses follow the noun directly and start with 'who', 'that', or 'which'.

options: Renting an inflatable mattress
The general upward pressure on rental prices, often driven in central neighbourhoods by conversion of housing stock to short-term tourist use
Inflating tyres at a rental property
Inflation that only affects landlords
mcQuestions.explanation: Rent inflation is the rise in rental prices — and the essay argues that mass conversion to short-term rentals is a measurable driver of it in central neighbourhoods.
mcQuestions.hint: What is inflation in economics, and what does it mean for rents specifically?

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence closes the essay with a sharp, summary verdict?
options: In conclusion, short-term rentals are complicated.
Therefore, all platforms should be banned globally.
The hosts who stay are then asked to live in the hotel.
To sum up, both residents and tourists have rights.
mcQuestions.explanation: Option C closes with a sharp paradox: when a neighbourhood becomes a hotel, the remaining residents have to live inside it — capturing the indignity the essay has been arguing against.
mcQuestions.hint: Which option ends with a memorable paradox about what residents become?

mcQuestions.prompt: According to the essay, why is a vague ban worse than a calibrated cap?
options: Because a vague ban is harder to enforce technically
Because a blanket ban punishes the small operators the policy should protect, while a street-level cap can grandfather them in and target conversion rates instead
Because vague bans are unconstitutional
Because vague bans always cost more money
mcQuestions.explanation: The essay argues that small elderly hosts and casual operators are exactly the kind of people a blanket ban hurts, while a calibrated cap can preserve their income and still protect residential density.
mcQuestions.hint: Look at paragraphs three and five for the writer's specific argument about who a blanket ban harms.

criticalThinkingQuestion: Think of a neighbourhood you know that has changed because of short-term rentals. What specifically has been lost — a shop, a friend, a name on a door, a sound at four in the morning? If you could write one clause into your city's housing law tomorrow, what would it protect, and from whom?
